title: Swap Two Variables
problem: Swap two variables without using a third variable. Return as array [b, a].
concept: Variables are containers. You can encode multiple values temporarily using arithmetic. Alternative: destructuring [a,b]=[b,a] or XOR for integers.
common_mistakes: Forgetting that addition can overflow for very large numbers. XOR approach (a^=b; b^=a; a^=b) avoids overflow but only works for integers.

title: Find the Maximum
problem: Find the largest number in an array without using Math.max.
concept: Linear scan visits every element once at O(n). Initialize with first element, not 0 or -Infinity, to handle all-negative arrays correctly. This pattern appears everywhere: finding min, max, first match, etc.
common_mistakes: Initializing max to 0 (fails for all-negative arrays). Using Math.max(...arr) which fails for very large arrays due to stack overflow.

title: Reverse a String
problem: Reverse a string without using .reverse().
concept: Iterating backwards is foundational. String concatenation in a loop is O(n²) in some languages but acceptable in JS. For performance, use array + join. Reversal shows up in linked lists, trees, and palindrome problems.
common_mistakes: Off-by-one on the loop bounds. Using s.split('').reverse().join('') defeats the purpose of the exercise.

title: Count Occurrences
problem: Count how many times a target value appears in an array.
concept: Accumulator pattern: running tally as you iterate. Same structure as summing, but increment by 1 on match instead of adding value. This is the basis of frequency counting which leads directly to hash map problems.
common_mistakes: Using == instead of === (type coercion). Not initializing count to 0.

title: FizzBuzz
problem: Return array 1 to n: multiples of 3→'Fizz', 5→'Buzz', both→'FizzBuzz', else the number.
concept: Conditional logic order matters: check most specific case first (divisible by 15) before checking individual divisors. Modulo operator (%) returns remainder. This tests basic control flow understanding.
common_mistakes: Checking %3 and %5 before %15 (the 'both' case never triggers). String concatenation approach ('Fizz'+'Buzz') works but is less clear.

title: Two Sum (Brute Force)
problem: Find two indices whose values add to target. Exactly one solution exists.
concept: Nested loop brute force is O(n²). Check every pair. In Tier 2 you optimize this to O(n) using a hash map — same problem, dramatically different performance. Understanding WHY brute force is slow is key to appreciating the hash map solution.
common_mistakes: Starting inner loop at 0 instead of i+1 (comparing element with itself). Not returning when found.

title: Sum of Array
problem: Return the sum of all numbers without .reduce().
concept: Accumulator pattern: identical to counting but adding values instead of incrementing. Initialize to 0 (identity element for addition). This is the basis of running sum, average, and prefix sum patterns.
common_mistakes: None common — this is fundamental. But understand that floating-point addition can introduce precision errors for decimal values.

title: Count Vowels
problem: Count vowels (a,e,i,o,u) in a string. Case insensitive.
concept: Membership testing — check if character belongs to a set. Using a string with .includes() is a simplified hash lookup. For larger sets, use a Set or object for O(1) lookup instead of O(n) string scan.
common_mistakes: Forgetting to lowercase. Not handling uppercase vowels.

title: Remove Duplicates
problem: Return new array with duplicates removed, preserving order. No Set.
concept: O(n²) brute force: for each element, check if already in result. In Tier 2, a Set gives O(n). Understanding the brute force first makes the optimization meaningful.
common_mistakes: Using indexOf which is cleaner but still O(n²). Using Set() defeats the learning purpose.

title: Factorial
problem: Calculate n! using a loop. 0!=1, 5!=120.
concept: Product accumulator: multiply running total instead of adding. Shows up in permutations (n!) and combinations (n choose k). Base case 0!=1 is a mathematical convention.
common_mistakes: Forgetting base case 0!=1. Initializing product to 0 instead of 1.

title: Reverse Array In Place
problem: Reverse array in place (modify original). Return it.
concept: Two-pointer converging: swap from edges inward. O(n) time, O(1) space — true in-place. This technique appears in palindrome checking, partitioning, and the triple-reverse rotation trick.
common_mistakes: Using l <= r instead of l < r (works but does unnecessary swap on odd-length middle element).

title: Is Anagram
problem: Check if two strings are anagrams. Case insensitive.
concept: Frequency counting with an object IS a hash map. Count up from first string, count down from second. If all zeros at end, anagram. This is the same pattern used in Group Anagrams (Tier 2) and many hash map problems.
complexity: O(n) time, O(k) space where k = alphabet size
common_mistakes: Sorting both strings and comparing works but is O(n log n). Forgetting case normalization. Not handling spaces depends on problem requirements.

title: Find Second Largest
problem: Find the second largest number without sorting.
concept: Track multiple running values in one pass. When you find a new max, the old max becomes second. Edge case: duplicate max values.
common_mistakes: Not handling duplicates. Sorting works but is O(n log n).

title: Capitalize Words
problem: Capitalize the first letter of each word.
tests: capitalizeWords('hello world')→'Hello World', capitalizeWords('a b c')→'A B C'
concept: State machine pattern: boolean flag tracks whether you're at the start of a new word. State transitions on space characters. This is how parsers and tokenizers work at a basic level.
common_mistakes: Using split/map/join is cleaner but misses the state machine learning opportunity.

title: Array Intersection
problem: Return elements common to both arrays with matching duplicate count.
concept: Frequency count one array, consume from the other. O(n+m) time. Decrementing prevents over-counting duplicates.
common_mistakes: Not decrementing the count (duplicates counted multiple times). Nested loop approach is O(n*m).

title: Running Sum
problem: Each element equals the sum of all elements up to that index (prefix sum).
concept: Prefix sum: each output = previous output + current input. After computing, any subarray sum can be calculated in O(1) using prefix[j] - prefix[i-1]. Foundation for range query optimization.
common_mistakes: Off-by-one starting the loop. Not handling single-element arrays.

title: Find Missing Number
problem: Array has n distinct numbers from 0 to n. Find the missing one.
concept: Mathematical insight: expected sum (Gauss's formula n*(n+1)/2) minus actual sum = missing number. O(n) time, O(1) space. Alternative: XOR all indices and values. Shows math can replace brute force.
common_mistakes: Not knowing Gauss's formula. Sorting then scanning is O(n log n). Using a Set is O(n) space.

title: Power of Two
problem: Is the number a power of 2? Handle n <= 0.
concept: Bit manipulation: powers of 2 have exactly one set bit. n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. If result is 0, only one bit was set → power of 2. Audio relevance: buffer sizes and FFT lengths must be powers of 2.
common_mistakes: Not handling n=0 (0 & -1 = 0 which would falsely return true). Not handling negative numbers.

title: Rotate Array
problem: Rotate right by k positions in place. [1,2,3,4,5] k=2 → [4,5,1,2,3].
concept: Triple-reverse trick: reverse whole array, reverse first k, reverse rest. O(n) time, O(1) space. Must handle k > array length with modulo. This trick shows up in string rotation problems too.
common_mistakes: Forgetting k % arr.length. Using extra array (O(n) space). Off-by-one in reverse bounds.

title: Compress String
problem: Run-length encode: 'aabcccccaaa'→'a2b1c5a3'. Return original if not shorter.
concept: Run-length encoding — actual compression used in audio (PCM silence), images (BMP), and data transmission. Count consecutive identical characters. When character changes, emit char+count and reset.
common_mistakes: Not resetting counter. Not comparing compressed vs original length. Off-by-one at end of string.
